arguments.old_string:
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log. In old versions of the record format * (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but * could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally * contain many records regardless of compression. * *

This interface provides access to batch metadata (offsets, timestamps, producer information, * compression type) and an iterator over the individual {@link Record}s contained in the batch. * *

Thread Safety: Implementations are generally not thread-safe. The calling code must * ensure that batches are not accessed concurrently from multiple threads. However, read-only access * to immutable batch data is safe. * *

Protocol Role: RecordBatch abstracts the Kafka record format (currently magic v0 through v2). * It handles serialization and deserialization of record batches in the on-disk and on-wire format, * which is critical for: *

    *
  • Producer clients sending records to brokers
    • *
  • Brokers storing records in the log
    • *
  • Consumer clients reading records from the log
    • *
  • Brokers validating CRC checksums for data integrity
    • *
* *

Compression Support: This interface supports multiple compression types (GZIP, Snappy, LZ4, ZSTD). * Decompression is transparent to callers using the {@link #iterator()} or {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}. * * @see {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for the magic v2+ implementation * @see {@link Record} for individual records within a batch * @see {@link MemoryRecords} for a collection of record batches in memory */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

arguments.old_string:
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
arguments.new_string:
/** * Concrete implementation of {@link RecordBatch} for magic v2 and above. * *

Purpose: This class handles serialization and deserialization of record batches in * the Kafka message format v2. It implements the {@link MutableRecordBatch} interface to support * in-place modification of batch metadata after creation (e.g., during log compaction or timestamp * updates). * *

Thread Safety: NOT thread-safe. The internal {@link ByteBuffer} is not * synchronized. Callers must ensure that instances are not accessed concurrently from multiple threads. * However, methods are safe to call sequentially from a single thread. * *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Batch header access (magic, compression, offset, etc.) is O(1) and very fast as it reads * directly from the buffer without iteration.
    • *
  • Record iteration is lazy - the {@link #iterator()} method returns an iterator that * only decompresses records as needed.
    • *
  • {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} provides memory-efficient decompression * using a supplied buffer, useful for processing very large batches.
    • *
  • {@link #skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier)} is optimized to iterate records while * skipping key/value payload data, useful for log validation.
    • *
  • Checksum validation is performed via {@link #isValid()} and can be deferred until needed.
    • *
* *

Log Compaction Considerations: This class preserves the original batch's base offset * and sequence numbers even when records are removed during log compaction. This is critical for * idempotent producer deduplication and transaction consistency. Empty batches may be retained * to preserve producer sequence state. * *

Batch Format Schema: * * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:

/** * Gets the base timestamp of the batch, which is used as the reference point for calculating * individual record timestamps from their deltas. * *

The base timestamp represents the timestamp of the first record in the original batch (before * any log compaction). During compaction, if all records are removed, the base timestamp is set to * {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} (-1). If the delete horizon flag is set, this value represents * the timestamp at which tombstones and aborted transaction markers should be removed. * *

Performance: O(1) direct buffer read. * * @return The base timestamp in milliseconds, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} if not set */ public long baseTimestamp() {

/** * Creates an input stream for reading the records from this batch, handling decompression if needed. * *

This method supports all compression types (GZIP, Snappy, LZ4, ZSTD). For uncompressed batches, * the returned stream reads directly from the buffer. For compressed batches, the stream transparently * decompresses the data as it is read. * *

Performance: O(1) for uncompressed batches. For compressed batches, the cost depends on * the decompression algorithm and the size of the data. The supplied {@link BufferSupplier} is used * to allocate decompression buffers, allowing callers to control memory usage and reuse buffers. * * @param bufferSupplier A supplier that provides {@link ByteBuffer}s for decompression. Using a * reusable supplier significantly improves performance for small batches. * @return An input stream positioned at the start of the records data * @throws java.io.UnsupportedOperationException if the compression type is not supported */ public InputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate(); buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET); return Compression.of(compressionType()).build().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier); }

/** * Returns a closeable iterator that reads records while skipping key, value, and header payloads. * *

This iterator is optimized for scenarios where only record metadata (offsets, timestamps, etc.) * is needed, such as: *

    *
  • Log validation and integrity checking
    • *
  • Truncation point detection
    • *
  • Offset range scanning without full record access
    • *
* *

Memory Efficiency: For compressed batches, this iterator decompresses incrementally * without buffering the entire decompressed data, using the supplied {@link BufferSupplier} for * temporary buffers. This provides significant memory savings compared to full record iteration. * *

Compressed vs Uncompressed: For uncompressed batches, this method returns the standard * uncompressed iterator, as skipping is not more efficient than reading in that case. * *

Important: Callers MUST invoke {@link CloseableIterator#close()} when done to release * any decompression resources (especially important for compressed batches). * *

Performance: O(1) for uncompressed batches. For compressed batches, O(n) where n is * the number of records, with reduced memory consumption compared to full decompression. * * @param bufferSupplier A supplier providing {@link ByteBuffer}s for decompression. A reusable * supplier significantly improves performance. * @return A closeable iterator over records with payloads skipped. Must be closed by caller. */ @Override public CloseableIterator skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { if (count() == 0) { return CloseableIterator.wrap(Collections.emptyIterator()); } /* * For uncompressed iterator, it is actually not worth skipping key / value / headers at all since * its ByteBufferInputStream's skip() function is less efficient compared with just reading it actually * as it will allocate new byte array. */ if (!isCompressed()) return uncompressedIterator(); // we define this to be a closable iterator so that caller (i.e. the log validator) needs to close it // while we can save memory footprint of not decompressing the full record set ahead of time return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, true); }

/** * Returns a closeable iterator that streams records with lazy decompression. * *

This method provides memory-efficient record iteration for compressed batches by decompressing * records only as they are accessed via {@link CloseableIterator#next()}. This is particularly * valuable for: *

    *
  • Processing very large record batches with limited memory
    • *
  • Consumer clients reading records from the log
    • *
  • Broker-side record filtering and transformation
    • *
* *

Decompression Buffer Reuse: The supplied {@link BufferSupplier} allows callers to * provide pre-allocated buffers for decompression, which significantly improves performance for * small batches (where buffer allocation overhead is significant) or when processing many batches * sequentially (where buffer reuse avoids repeated allocations). * *

Uncompressed Batches: For uncompressed batches, returns a lightweight iterator that * reads directly from the batch's buffer. * *

Important: Callers MUST invoke {@link CloseableIterator#close()} when done to release * any decompression resources. This is especially critical for compressed batches to release * InputStreams and decompression buffers. * *

Performance: For compressed batches, decompression cost is amortized across individual * record reads. Using a reusable {@link BufferSupplier} is important for achieving good performance, * especially for LZ4 (64KB default buffer). * * @param bufferSupplier A supplier providing {@link ByteBuffer}s for decompression. For optimal * performance, provide a supplier that reuses buffers. A value of * {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} works but has worse performance. * @return A closeable iterator over records. Must be closed by caller. * @see org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier for guidance on buffer supplier strategies */ @Override public CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { if (isCompressed()) return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false); else return uncompressedIterator(); }

/** * Sets the base offset of this batch, which combined with the last offset delta determines * the last offset of the batch. * *

Log Compaction Context: This method is used during log compaction to update the * base offset of a batch after records have been removed. The base offset and offset deltas * together define the offset range of the batch and are critical for the log index and * consumer offset management. * *

Thread Safety: NOT thread-safe. Callers must ensure exclusive access to this * instance during modification. * *

Performance: O(1) direct buffer write. * * @param offset The new last offset (inclusive). The base offset is computed as * {@code offset - lastOffsetDelta()} * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the resulting offset is negative */ @Override public void setLastOffset(long offset) { buffer.putLong(BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET, offset - lastOffsetDelta()); }

/** * Sets the partition leader epoch for this batch. * *

Context: The partition leader epoch is assigned by the broker when the batch is * appended to the log. It is not included in the CRC computation, allowing the epoch to be * assigned without requiring CRC recalculation. This is important for performance as batches * are appended to the log on every broker request. * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Replica synchronization: ensures replicas have the correct epoch for the partition
    • *
  • Leader failure detection: helps identify and remove data from old leader epochs
    • *
  • Boundary detection: used to identify data boundaries when replicas need to truncate
    • *
* *

Thread Safety: NOT thread-safe. Callers must ensure exclusive access to this * instance during modification. * *

Performance: O(1) direct buffer write. Does not require CRC recomputation. * * @param epoch The partition leader epoch, typically assigned by the broker. A value of * {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH} (-1) indicates unknown epoch. */ @Override public void setPartitionLeaderEpoch(int epoch) { buffer.putInt(PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET, epoch); }

arguments.new_string:
/** * Writes a batch header for an empty batch (zero records) to the provided buffer. * *

Purpose: Empty batches are used during log compaction to preserve producer state * (sequence numbers) even when all records in a batch are removed. This prevents subsequent * duplicate messages from the same producer being incorrectly marked as duplicates. * *

Performance: O(1) buffer writes. No record iteration or compression. * * @param buffer The buffer to write the empty batch header to. Must have at least * {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes remaining. * @param magic The record format version (must be {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} * or higher) * @param producerId The producer ID. Use {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} (-1) if not applicable. * @param producerEpoch The producer epoch. Use {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} (-1) if not applicable. * @param baseSequence The base sequence number of the original batch. Use * {@link RecordBatch#NO_SEQUENCE} (-1) if not applicable. * @param baseOffset The base (first) offset of this batch * @param lastOffset The last (inclusive) offset of this batch. For an empty batch, this is * typically equal to baseOffset. * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch. Use * {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH} (-1) if unknown. * @param timestampType The timestamp type ({@link TimestampType#CREATE_TIME} or * {@link TimestampType#LOG_APPEND_TIME}) * @param timestamp The timestamp to set. For empty batches created during compaction, this * is typically the delete horizon timestamp. * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlRecord Whether this batch contains control records * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parameters are invalid */ public static void writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, byte magic, long producerId, short producerEpoch, int baseSequence, long baseOffset, long lastOffset, int partitionLeaderEpoch, TimestampType timestampType, long timestamp, boolean isTransactional, boolean isControlRecord) {

arguments.old_string:
public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                   long baseOffset,
                                   int lastOffsetDelta,
                                   int sizeInBytes,
                                   byte magic,
                                   CompressionType compressionType,
                                   TimestampType timestampType,
                                   long baseTimestamp,
                                   long maxTimestamp,
                                   long producerId,
                                   short epoch,
                                   int sequence,
                                   boolean isTransactional,
                                   boolean isControlBatch,
                                   boolean isDeleteHorizonSet,
                                   int partitionLeaderEpoch,
                                   int numRecords) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Writes a complete batch header to the provided buffer, including the CRC. * *

Purpose: This is the main method for constructing record batches during message * production and log compaction. The header contains all metadata for the batch, followed by * the serialized records. * *

CRC Computation: The CRC is computed over all batch data from the attributes field * onwards (after the CRC itself), using the CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial for fast, * hardware-accelerated computation when available. * *

Offset Handling: The {@code lastOffsetDelta} is computed as the difference between * the last and base offsets. This allows efficient storage of large offset ranges while * preserving exact offset information. * *

Performance: O(batch size) due to CRC computation over the batch data. * * @param buffer The buffer to write the header to. Must have at least * {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} + record data bytes available. * @param baseOffset The base (first) offset of this batch * @param lastOffsetDelta The difference between the last and base offsets (lastOffset - baseOffset). * Used for efficient offset range representation. * @param sizeInBytes Total size of the batch including header and records, in bytes. * Must include {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} for the header. * @param magic The record format version. Currently supports * {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (2) or above. * @param compressionType The compression algorithm ({@link CompressionType#NONE}, * {@link CompressionType#GZIP}, etc.) * @param timestampType The timestamp type ({@link TimestampType#CREATE_TIME} or * {@link TimestampType#LOG_APPEND_TIME}) * @param baseTimestamp The timestamp of the first record in the batch, used as the reference * for timestamp deltas. Use {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} (-1) if * the batch is empty. * @param maxTimestamp The maximum timestamp in the batch, or the broker-assigned timestamp * if using LOG_APPEND_TIME * @param producerId The producer ID. Use {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} (-1) if not applicable. * @param epoch The producer epoch. Use {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} (-1) if not applicable. * @param sequence The base sequence number. Use {@link RecordBatch#NO_SEQUENCE} (-1) if not applicable. * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlBatch Whether this batch contains control records (e.g., transaction markers) * @param isDeleteHorizonSet Whether the delete horizon flag is set (for log compaction) * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch. Use * {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH} (-1) if unknown. * @param numRecords The number of records in this batch. Must match the actual count of * records in the batch data. * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic, timestamp, or other parameters are invalid */ public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, long baseOffset, int lastOffsetDelta, int sizeInBytes, byte magic, CompressionType compressionType, TimestampType timestampType, long baseTimestamp, long maxTimestamp, long producerId, short epoch, int sequence, boolean isTransactional, boolean isControlBatch, boolean isDeleteHorizonSet, int partitionLeaderEpoch, int numRecords) {

/** * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a {@link ByteBuffer}, providing in-memory access to * Kafka record batches. * *

Purpose: MemoryRecords is used for: *

    *
  • Reading: Parsing and iterating over record batches in a buffer (e.g., from the network * in consumer or broker clients)
    • *
  • In-place Modification: Updating batch metadata without copying data (e.g., during * log compaction or timestamp updates)
    • *
  • Filtering: Selectively retaining records based on custom filters (e.g., for log * cleanup and compaction)
    • *
* *

Construction: Use one of the factory methods to create instances: *

    *
  • {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)} - Create a read-only view of existing buffer data
    • *
  • {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, Compression, TimestampType, long)} - Create a builder for * writing new records (recommended for production)
    • *
  • {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)} - Convenient factory for simple cases
    • *
* *

Thread Safety: NOT thread-safe. The underlying {@link ByteBuffer} is not * synchronized. Concurrent access from multiple threads is unsafe. However: *

    *
  • Multiple readers can safely iterate over the same MemoryRecords instance from a single * thread, as long as the buffer is not modified
    • *
  • Read operations are safe as long as the buffer is not concurrently modified
    • *
  • For concurrent access, external synchronization is required
    • *
* *

Buffer Ownership: MemoryRecords does not own the underlying ByteBuffer. Callers are * responsible for managing buffer lifecycle. The buffer must remain valid for the lifetime of * the MemoryRecords instance. * *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • {@link #batches()} is lazy - returns an iterable that only allocates for batch iteration * when needed
    • *
  • Batch iteration is O(n) where n is the number of batches
    • *
  • Record iteration within a batch is lazy for compressed batches (decompression on demand)
    • *
  • {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} provides efficient filtering * without re-copying unmodified batches
    • *
  • {@link #validBytes()} iterates all batches on first call, then caches the result
    • *
* *

Batch Format Support: Supports magic versions 0, 1, and 2+ with automatic routing * to the appropriate batch implementation ({@link DefaultRecordBatch} for magic v2). * * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for building new record batches * @see DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2 batch implementation * @see RecordBatch for the batch interface */ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {

/** * Returns the total number of complete record bytes, excluding any partial or trailing data. * *

The number of valid bytes represents the total size of all complete record batches. * This may be smaller than {@link #sizeInBytes()} if the buffer contains incomplete batch * data at the end (e.g., due to network fragmentation or truncated buffers). * *

Caching: The result is cached after the first call. Subsequent calls return * the cached value without recomputing. This is safe because MemoryRecords is typically * immutable after creation. * *

Performance: First call is O(number of batches) and may be expensive for large * buffers with many batches. Subsequent calls are O(1). * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Determining how much of the buffer contains valid, complete batches
    • *
  • Detecting truncated or incomplete batches in the buffer
    • *
  • Calculating the amount of data to write or replicate
    • *
* * @return The number of valid complete bytes in the buffer */ public int validBytes() {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Validates the header of the first batch and returns batch size.
     * @return first batch size including LOG_OVERHEAD if buffer contains header up to
     *         magic byte, null otherwise
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if record size or magic is invalid
     */
    public Integer firstBatchSize() {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Validates and returns the size of the first batch in the buffer. * *

This method reads and validates the batch header up to the magic byte to determine the * batch size. It is useful for: *

    *
  • Detecting incomplete batches (returns null if insufficient header data)
    • *
  • Validating that the buffer contains a valid batch at the start
    • *
  • Determining how much data needs to be read to process the first batch
    • *
* *

Validation: Validates that the batch header is well-formed, including: *

    *
  • Sufficient buffer space for the header
    • *
  • Valid record size (reasonable bounds)
    • *
  • Valid magic version
    • *
* *

Performance: O(1) - reads and validates only the header. * *

Return Value: *

    *
  • Non-null: The total size of the first batch including {@link org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal.Records#LOG_OVERHEAD} * in bytes
    • *
  • Null: The buffer does not contain a complete batch header (needs more data)
    • *
* * @return The size in bytes of the first complete batch (including LOG_OVERHEAD), or * null if insufficient data for header * @throws CorruptRecordException if the batch header is invalid (bad magic or record size) */ public Integer firstBatchSize() {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Filters records using a custom {@link RecordFilter} and writes results to a destination buffer. * *

Purpose: This method is used for: *

    *
  • Log Compaction: Removing obsolete (tombstone) records and preserving batch state
    • *
  • Record Filtering: Removing records based on custom retention policies
    • *
  • Data Transformation: Converting record format or updating batch metadata
    • *
  • Performance Metrics: Collecting statistics on records processed and retained
    • *
* *

Filtering Process: For each batch: *

    *
  1. Calls {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} to determine batch-level action
    2. *
  2. If batch is deleted, skips it entirely
    3. *
  3. If batch is retained, iterates records calling * {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)}
    4. *
  4. Retained records are written to the destination buffer
    5. *
  5. Updates batch metadata (timestamps, offsets) as needed for compaction
    6. *
  6. Empties batches are optionally retained to preserve producer state
    7. *
* *

Compression Handling: Compressed batches are transparently decompressed during * filtering, and the filtered output may be re-compressed depending on the filter implementation. * *

Buffer Overflow: If the destination buffer overflows, an overflow buffer is * automatically allocated and returned in the {@link FilterResult}. Callers must handle * potential buffer changes. * *

Memory Efficiency: The {@code decompressionBufferSupplier} is critical for * memory-efficient operation: *

    *
  • Use a reusable supplier (e.g., {@link BufferSupplier#create()}) to reuse buffers * across batches
    • *
  • Avoid {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} unless memory is very constrained
    • *
  • For LZ4 (64KB default) or large batches, buffer reuse has significant performance * impact
    • *
* *

Performance: O(number of records) for iteration. Compression/decompression cost * depends on the algorithms used and reuse of decompression buffers. * * @param filter The filter logic determining which batches and records to retain. Must not * be null. {@link RecordFilter} is an abstract class that must be subclassed. * @param destinationBuffer The buffer to write filtered records to. Must be writable and * have sufficient space or able to be grown. May be returned * unmodified or as a different buffer in the result. * @param decompressionBufferSupplier Supplies buffers for decompression. Using a reusable * supplier is important for memory efficiency, especially * for LZ4 compression. Consider using * {@link BufferSupplier#create()} or similar. * @return A {@link FilterResult} containing: *

    *
  • The output buffer (may differ from destinationBuffer if overflow)
    • *
  • Metrics: messages/bytes read, retained, and maximum offset/timestamp
    • *
*/ public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {

/** * Returns a duplicate view of the underlying buffer for reading. * *

The returned buffer is a duplicate, not the original internal buffer. Modifications to * the returned buffer's position/limit will not affect the internal state. However, they * share the same backing byte data (not a deep copy). * *

Thread Safety: The returned buffer is independent of the internal buffer's * position/limit, but both share the same data. External synchronization is still required * if the underlying data might be modified. * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Direct buffer access for custom serialization
    • *
  • Writing buffer contents to channels or streams
    • *
  • Inspecting raw byte data
    • *
* *

Performance: O(1) - creates a duplicate buffer view without copying data. * * @return A duplicate of the backing ByteBuffer, positioned at the start and ready for reading */ public ByteBuffer buffer() { return buffer.duplicate(); }

/** * Returns an iterable over all record batches in this buffer. * *

Lazy Iteration: The batches are parsed lazily as the iterator is consumed. * Calling this method does not iterate or validate all batches; iteration happens on-demand * as the iterator advances. * *

Multiple Iterations: The returned iterable can be used to iterate multiple times. * Each iteration will re-parse the batches from the buffer. * *

Modification During Iteration: The buffer must not be modified while iteration * is in progress. Modification may cause iteration to fail or produce inconsistent results. * *

Batch Types: Returns {@link MutableRecordBatch} instances, allowing batch * metadata to be updated in-place (if needed for log compaction). * *

Performance: O(1) to obtain the iterable. O(n) to iterate over all n batches, * with lazy parsing cost distributed across iterations. * * @return An {@link Iterable} over {@link MutableRecordBatch} instances */ @Override public Iterable batches() { return batches; }

/** * Creates a builder for constructing new record batches with the current magic version. * *

Recommended Factory: This is the most commonly used builder factory, suitable for: *

    *
  • Producer clients creating batches to send to brokers
    • *
  • Creating new batches during log compaction
    • *
  • General-purpose batch construction
    • *
* *

Defaults: Uses the following defaults: *

    *
  • Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (currently v2)
    • *
  • Producer ID: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} (-1, non-idempotent)
    • *
  • Transactional: false (not part of a transaction)
    • *
  • Log append time: set based on {@code timestampType}
    • *
  • Partition leader epoch: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH} (-1)
    • *
* *

Performance: The builder lazily allocates batch memory and compresses on close(). * * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write batch data to. Must be writable and have sufficient * remaining space for the batch(es). * @param compression The compression algorithm to use. Use {@link Compression#NONE} for * uncompressed batches. * @param timestampType The timestamp type: {@link TimestampType#CREATE_TIME} (producer timestamp) * or {@link TimestampType#LOG_APPEND_TIME} (broker assigned). * @param baseOffset The starting offset for records in this batch * @return A {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} for appending records * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder#append(SimpleRecord) to add records */ public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer, Compression compression, TimestampType timestampType, long baseOffset) { return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset); }

/** * Creates a {@link MemoryRecords} instance for reading batches from an existing buffer. * *

Purpose: This is the factory for creating read-only or in-place-modifiable * MemoryRecords from an existing buffer containing serialized record batches. * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Consumer clients: Create from network buffers received from brokers
    • *
  • Broker-side: Create from log segments for filtering or replication
    • *
  • In-place modification: Update batch metadata without copying (see * {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)})
    • *
* *

Buffer Ownership: The caller retains ownership of the buffer. MemoryRecords * does not copy the buffer or manage its lifecycle. The buffer must remain valid for the * lifetime of the returned MemoryRecords instance. * *

Thread Safety: The returned instance is not thread-safe. External * synchronization is required for concurrent access. * *

Performance: O(1) - does not parse or validate batches until iteration. * * @param buffer A ByteBuffer containing serialized record batch(es) in Kafka format. * The buffer position and limit determine the data range. * @return A {@link MemoryRecords} instance for reading or modifying the batches */ public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) { return new MemoryRecords(buffer); }
